q: What is the WARN Act and does it apply to me?
a: The WARN Act requires 60 days notice for layoffs affecting 50+ employees at companies with 100+ workers. If violated, you may be owed back pay and benefits for those 60 days.

q: How long do I have to review and sign a severance agreement?
a: You typically have 21 days to review a severance agreement (45 days if it's part of a group layoff). After signing, you have 7 days to revoke. Never sign on the spot.

Non-compete clauses are unenforceable in California, North Dakota, Oklahoma, and Minnesota (as of 2023). In other states enforceability depends on scope, duration, and state law — consult an employment attorney.

q: How long can I keep my health insurance after a layoff?
a: COBRA lets you continue your employer health plan for up to 18 months. You must elect within 60 days of losing coverage. You pay the full premium (employee + employer share) plus a 2% admin fee.

q: Am I eligible for unemployment insurance?
a: You're eligible if you were laid off through no fault of your own. Apply the same week as your layoff — most states don't back-pay the first week. Benefits typically replace 40-50% of prior wages up to a state maximum.

q: Can my employer claw back a signing bonus after a layoff?
a: If your offer letter included a clawback clause tied to a minimum tenure, the employer may try to recover it. Review your offer letter and consult an attorney — clawbacks are often negotiable.

q: What is a release of claims and should I sign it?
a: A release of claims waives your right to sue the company (e.g., for discrimination or WARN Act violations) in exchange for severance. Review with an employment attorney before signing — you can negotiate the terms.

q: Can I negotiate my severance package?
a: Yes. Common negotiables: additional weeks of pay, extended healthcare, accelerated vesting, outplacement services, and removal of non-disparagement clauses. Negotiate before signing the release.
